speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Rose Rotkelichen Minsky, R-O-S-E R-O-T-K-E-L-I-C-H-E-N, Minsky, M-I-N-S-K-Y. Her date of birth is October 28th, 1919, and she was born in Warsaw, Poland. I, Leslie Bennett Troper, am the interviewer. And we are in Brooklyn, New York, in the United States of America. Today's date is December 7th, 1994. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] At this time, Mrs. Minsky, will you please introduce this gentleman here with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Sam, who came with me to this country in 1948 at the age of 10 months. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He lives now in California, in Santa Monica, and he works for the [? Medic ?] Center, and he came here for a visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sam, how do you feel about what your mother is doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was reading the and I saw an article that Steven Spielberg was funding some kind of archives of the memoirs of survivors of the Holocaust, and I thought of my mother. I know she's been doing a lot of work all her life, in terms of lectures and public discussions and going to schools and things like that, to let the world know as much as she can about what happened and what it was like in Europe for the Jews and the other people who perished at the hands of the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I called her up and let her know that I was going to submit her name, and that's how this interview came about. And I'd like to say that I'm very proud of my mother for having survived and-- and the terrible things that she went through. And also for the work that she's done and continues to do in this area.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I also want to say that I think it's very important, for me personally, to have learned and understood what happened to my parents in Europe at that time. Because my life has also been greatly impacted by their experiences in-- in many ways that I could not even understand until very recently, the-- the great effect that it's had on me and my other siblings, as well in how we perceive the world and how we have experienced our lives. So I'm very happy that she's doing this today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has there been anything that you have done in your life as a result of your mother's experiences and all that you've learned from her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, at the age of 40, I went back to graduate school after almost a 20-year absence of going to school. And I got a degree in family therapy in California. I'm a licensed psychotherapist out there. And as part of my master's thesis I compiled about eight hours of audiotape on both my parents-- on their experiences from their first memories until they came to America in 1948 when I was an infant-- in as much detail as I could get them to remember, which was actually quite a bit. And-- and this thesis that I wrote-- actually it was a 400-page document-- but it was a-- it was a-- a great growth experience for me, in terms of understanding how my life had also been indelibly marked by the Holocaust even though I didn't experience it personally. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Germany, in Bensheim, in a DP camp. This is a camp for displaced persons, because we were at that time considered as people without a country, waiting there for our next-- to-- to get permitted to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about what your son has chosen to do as a professional, and the choice of subjects that he--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very proud of him. And I know he lived through this, although he was not there at that time, but he-- like, he feels like he's a part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you involved in any organizations at this time that hold on to the Holocaust experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at this time I'm not. I-- I went-- years ago, I went to a couple of meetings of Second Generation. And for personal reasons at that time, I decided not to become more involved with them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Minsky, will you please introduce your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Nathan. We married after the war. We met-- we met in [NON-ENGLISH]. And-- and we were on a DP camp. My son was born there, and we came to this country 1948. He started working very, very hard in the beginning as a presser. And then went to school and learned the language, and became-- and started working for the in Manhattan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Minsky, what do you think about what your wife is doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you the truth, I never heard her so much [? accurately ?] tell her story. We're married 50 years, and she's telling me and the children all the time certain fragments. But this time, thank to you and to the crew, that she gave you the detailed story what happened. So it's not much for me to add. What happened with the Jewry in Warsaw, what happened with people in concentration camps of Majdanek and Auschwitz, she was a witness there. She lived across the [? gas chambers ?]. And that we're nearing-- our generations, we're getting less, less people of survivors. And, as she told you, that we live in a bad world of wars. Still, the wars are going on. I don't want to go into politics, but [BACKGROUND NOISE] a lot of deniers came out, people with education, among all-- all nationalities, not only Germans. We have it here in America, too. Some people-- I don't want to mention, again, any names-- that they say the Jewish people, they never had a Holocaust. Never anything happened. And our job, together with you, with all humanity, is to achieve such a world that these things should-- of genocide, complete genocide should never take place to any race, [BACKGROUND NOISE] nationality, creed, color.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or religion.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all what I got to add.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank both of you so much for taking the time to share your feelings, to share your experiences with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm glad that I can-- can help for the future generations, that they should learn something.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing what I could add, I came out a terribly invalid, especially when I'm nearing my old age. I'm 78, 77. I stopped walking because probably [BACKGROUND NOISE] they broke my hip. They broke my hand. But we have the spirit. She's days before an operation maybe. It's not an operation because of her experiences. She needs cataract, but she has other illnesses. But we achieved something today. And especially I was proud that my son was here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was the initiator.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That we should testify to people whoever will watch us, or whoever will hear our voices. Maybe we wouldn't be around. They should remember, they should be living witnesses, as long as we're moving around with our bodies, with our spirits, and souls that this thing happened. And the world is still silent when people in races and creeds getting killed for no reason, working people like her father, like my-- plain-- plain people that toil day by day just to have bread. They were persecuted for no reason. And I'm glad that the Jewish people finally has their own country of Israel. If anything happens there, at least they're given a resistance with weapons. And they struggling for us and we are strong for them, that we should walk around with our heads up. We and other peoples of the world. No killings. No genocides. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my last statement, I mean, to you. I'm active in all these organizations, I would say, perpetuating Jewish culture and Yiddish culture, Hebrew culture. We're doing as much as we can to help, to continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who you think gonna see this?

speaker: interviewer
text: We thank you very much.
